seo.title: Phuket Travel Guide: Everything You Need to Know (2026)
seo.metaDescription: Complete Phuket travel guide for 2026. 5-day itinerary, flights from Bangkok, best beaches, local food guide, budget breakdown, and scam warnings.

intro: Phuket works on every level — five days gives you time for island hopping, beach exploring, temple visits, and serious eating. This guide covers the practical stuff: how to get there, how to get around (and avoid getting ripped off), what to eat, and how much to budget. Whether it's your first time or your tenth, there's always something new on Thailand's biggest island.

itinerary.title: 5 Days in Phuket: Beach, Island & Culture Itinerary

days.title: Patong Beach & Bangla Road
activities: Morning: Settle in and hit Patong Beach — rent a sunbed (200 THB) and get your bearings
Lunch: Beach-side restaurant on the promenade — stick to Thai food (cheaper and better than Western options)
Afternoon: Walk to Jungceylon Mall for air-conditioned shopping and a supermarket run for water and snacks
Sunset: Head to a rooftop bar in Patong for sunset cocktails — KEE Sky Lounge has great views
Evening: Explore Bangla Road walking street — the neon-lit epicenter of Phuket nightlife

days.title: Big Buddha, Wat Chalong & Old Town
activities: Morning: Visit Wat Chalong (free) — Phuket's most important temple, climb the Grand Pagoda for views
Mid-morning: Drive 10 minutes to Big Buddha (free) — 45-meter marble statue with 360° island panorama
Lunch: Head to Phuket Old Town for mee hokkien at Mee Ton Po (60–100 THB)
Afternoon: Walk Thalang Road and Soi Romanee — colorful Sino-Portuguese shophouses and street art
Late afternoon: Thai Hua Museum (200 THB) for context on Phuket's Chinese-Thai heritage
Evening: If Sunday — Old Town Walking Street Market (4–10 PM) for the island's best street food

days.title: Phi Phi Islands Day Trip
activities: Full day: Speedboat to Phi Phi Islands (1,500–3,000 THB including lunch and park fee)
Morning: Depart 7:30 AM from Rassada Pier — first stop Maya Bay (visitor cap, book ahead)
Midday: Snorkeling at Pileh Lagoon — crystal-clear water surrounded by limestone cliffs
Lunch: Buffet on Phi Phi Don (included in most tours)
Afternoon: Monkey Beach and additional snorkeling stops on the return
Evening: Recovery dinner at a quiet restaurant — try seafood at Rawai Market if you have energy

days.title: Beach Hopping & Sunset
activities: Morning: Rent a scooter (250–400 THB/day) and head to Nai Harn Beach — beautiful, less crowded
Mid-morning: Drive to Freedom Beach by the jungle trail (20-min hike) or longtail from Patong
Lunch: Seafood at Rawai Market — buy fresh fish and have it cooked across the street (300–600 THB feast)
Afternoon: Kata Beach for swimming (Nov–Apr) or surf lesson (Jun–Sep, 1,500 THB)
Sunset: Promthep Cape — Phuket's most famous viewpoint at the southern tip. Arrive 30 min early
Evening: Dinner at a Kata Beach restaurant with views

days.title: Phang Nga Bay or Relaxation Day
activities: Option A: Phang Nga Bay kayak & James Bond Island tour (1,500–3,500 THB) — paddle through sea caves and visit the floating village
Option B: Relaxation day — Thai massage at Kata (300 THB/hr), pool time at your hotel, last-minute shopping
Lunch: Kanom jeen (rice noodles with curry) at Phuket Town morning market if you're up early
Afternoon: Last beach session or visit Hanuman World zip-line (2,500–3,500 THB) for an adrenaline finale
Evening: Final sunset at your favorite viewpoint — Karon Viewpoint or Promthep Cape

transport.fromBangkok: Phuket International Airport (HKT) has dozens of daily flights from Bangkok — AirAsia, Nok Air, and Thai Lion Air from 1,500 THB one-way (1.5 hours). Thai Airways and Bangkok Airways offer premium service from 3,000 THB. There's no train to Phuket. VIP buses from Bangkok's Southern Bus Terminal take 12–14 hours (from 750 THB). Minivans from Surat Thani or Krabi take 3–4 hours (300–400 THB).
localTransport: Phuket Smart Bus: Airport to Rawai via all western beaches — 50–170 THB. The only public transport on the island
Grab/Bolt: Most reliable and fairly priced option — Patong to Kata 150–200 THB, Patong to airport 400–500 THB
Scooter rental: 250–400 THB/day. International driving permit technically required. The island is hilly — get 125cc minimum
Tuk-tuks (local vans): Overpriced at 400–600 THB for 15-minute rides. Always negotiate or use Grab instead
Car rental: From 1,000 THB/day with insurance. Recommended for families or groups — Phuket drives on the left
Airport taxi: Fixed price 600–800 THB to Patong, 500–600 THB to Kata. Book through the official counter, not touts

mustTry: Mee Hokkien — thick yellow noodles stir-fried with seafood and egg in rich soy sauce (Phuket's signature dish)
Moo Hong — braised pork belly in soy, garlic, and black pepper, slow-cooked until meltingly tender
Kanom Jeen — cold fermented rice noodles with fish curry and fresh herbs (morning-only dish)
Oh Tao — oyster-and-egg pancake fried crispy, a Hokkien Chinese-Phuket crossover snack
Lobster/crab at Rawai Market — buy fresh at the wet market, have it cooked across the street
Roti with curry — Indian-influenced flatbread served with Thai curries, best at Muslim food stalls
Yen Ta Fo — pink noodle soup with fermented soybean paste, tofu, and morning glory
foodAreas: Phuket Old Town — the epicenter of authentic Phuketian cuisine at local prices
Rawai Seafood Market — fresh seafood at wholesale prices, cooked to order
Old Town Sunday Walking Street Market — best street food concentration on the island
Banzaan Fresh Market, Patong — tourist-friendly wet market with upstairs food court
Kata Night Market — small but good selection of Thai street food near Kata Beach
tips: Phuket's cuisine is unique — heavily influenced by Hokkien Chinese and Malay traditions, different from central Thai food
Old Town food is 50–70% cheaper than beachfront restaurants and significantly better quality
Kanom jeen stalls close by 10 AM — set an alarm if you want to try this breakfast staple
Rawai Market is best visited around 5 PM when boats return with the freshest catch
Avoid restaurants right on Bangla Road — tourist trap pricing with mediocre food

etiquette: Cover shoulders and knees when visiting Wat Chalong and Big Buddha — sarongs available to borrow
Remove shoes before entering any temple building
Don't point your feet at Buddha images when sitting
Never touch a monk — women should step aside on narrow paths to let monks pass
Topless sunbathing is illegal on all Thai beaches — you can be fined
Stand during the national anthem played at 8 AM and 6 PM in public areas
Tipping is not expected but appreciated — 20–50 THB for restaurant meals, 50–100 THB for massage

budget.budget: 1,000–1,800 THB/day (~$28–50 USD): Hostel dorm 300–600 THB, street food meals 50–100 THB, local beach days (free), Smart Bus transport 50–170 THB, 1-hour massage 300 THB. Phuket is pricier than mainland Thailand but still very doable on a budget if you avoid Patong tourist traps.
budget.midRange: 3,000–6,000 THB/day (~$84–168 USD): Mid-range hotel 1,500–3,500 THB, mix of street food and restaurants 400–800 THB, one tour or activity 1,500–3,000 THB, Grab transport 200–400 THB. The sweet spot for most travelers.
budget.luxury: 10,000–30,000+ THB/day (~$280–840+ USD): Luxury resort or villa 5,000–20,000 THB, fine dining and beach clubs 2,000–5,000 THB, private island tours 5,000–15,000 THB, spa treatments 3,000–8,000 THB, private driver 2,000–3,000 THB/day.
